title: Safe Blood Transfusion Administration
subtitle: A patient is ordered one unit of packed red blood cells. The nurse verifies the unit safely, starts it correctly, monitors closely for the first 15 minutes, and knows exactly what to do if a transfusion reaction develops.

patient.name: Blood Transfusion
patient.admitDate: A patient is ordered one unit of packed red blood cells. The nurse safely verifies, administers, and monitors the transfusion and responds correctly to any reaction.

vitals.temp: ID + blood type + unit # at bedside

labs.v: Stop; NS via new line; notify.

nursesNote: A patient is ordered one unit of packed red blood cells. Before starting, the nurse confirms the provider order and informed consent, ensures large-bore IV access (18-20 gauge), obtains baseline vital signs, and performs a two-person bedside verification of patient identity, blood type, and unit number. Blood is administered through Y-type tubing with an in-line filter primed with 0.9% normal saline only — never dextrose or lactated Ringer's. The nurse starts the transfusion slowly, stays with the patient for the first 15 minutes (when most reactions appear), rechecks vital signs, and completes the unit within 4 hours. At the first sign of a reaction (fever, chills, flank/back pain, hypotension, dyspnea), the nurse STOPS the transfusion, keeps the line open with normal saline through new tubing, and notifies the provider and blood bank.
providerOrders: Confirm order and informed consent; ensure large-bore IV access
Obtain baseline vitals and perform two-person bedside verification
Administer through Y-tubing with a filter using 0.9% normal saline only
Start slowly, stay for the first 15 minutes, and complete within 4 hours
Stop immediately for any reaction; keep the line open with NS and notify

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about safe transfusion?

opts.text: Verify the order and confirm informed consent before transfusing
Two qualified staff verify patient ID, blood type, and unit number at the bedside
Only 0.9% normal saline is compatible with blood in the same line
Dextrose 5% and lactated Ringer's can be infused with blood
Obtain baseline vital signs before starting
A small 24-gauge IV is preferred for blood administration
Use Y-type tubing with an in-line filter for blood
Start slowly and stay with the patient for the first 15 minutes
Most acute reactions occur early in the transfusion
A single unit of PRBCs may safely hang for up to 8 hours
Blood should be started promptly after leaving the blood bank
Premedication is never appropriate for a patient with prior reactions

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to start a transfusion

opts.text: Verify the provider order and confirm informed consent
Obtain baseline vital signs and assess the patient
Perform two-person bedside verification of patient and unit
Prime Y-tubing with normal saline and begin slowly
Stay with the patient and recheck vitals at 15 minutes
Two-staff bedside verification of identity and unit
Hanging blood with lactated Ringer's
Using a 20-gauge IV and Y-tubing with a filter
Leaving the patient alone for the first 15 minutes
Taking baseline vital signs before starting
Infusing a single unit over 6 hours
Starting the transfusion at a slow rate initially
Adding an IV medication into the blood line

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: 20 min in: fever, chills, flank pain, falling BP
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop the transfusion immediately
Keep the IV open with normal saline through new tubing
Notify the provider and the blood bank
Recheck vital signs and monitor the patient
Return the blood and tubing, send samples, and document
Stopping the transfusion at the first sign of a reaction
Continuing through new back pain to finish the unit
Keeping the line open with normal saline after stopping
Flushing the suspected blood back in to clear the line
Verifying patient and unit with a second qualified staff member
Using dextrose solution to prime the blood tubing
Staying with the patient during the first 15 minutes
Hanging a single unit for more than 4 hours
